We are hiring business Development Manager

Role
We are looking for a high-energy, "go-getter" Business Development Manager to spearhead our growth. You will be the face of Appriffy, responsible for identifying new business opportunities, nurturing relationships, and closing deals with both Indian and international clients. This is a fast-paced role for someone who is hungry to achieve individual and teams’ targets and thrives in a performance-driven environment.

Key responsibilities
1. Prospecting: Identify and target potential clients (startups to enterprises) looking for high-calibre engineering talent or seeking to establish GCCs in India.
2. Lead Generation: Utilize various channels to build a robust sales pipeline and reach out to decision-makers globally.
3. Sales Pitching: Effectively communicate Appriffy’s value proposition including 4-day average fulfilment time and high 97% retention rate to prospective clients.
4. Follow-up: Maintain persistent and professional follow-ups to move prospects through the sales funnel.
5. Deal Closing: Negotiate and close contracts for month-to-month or long-term talent engagements to achieve individual and teams’ targets.
6. Market Intelligence: Stay updated on industry trends regarding remote engineering and the GCC landscape to better advise potential partners.
 
Must-Have Qualifications
1. Qualification: Graduate or MBA with 3 to 5 years of experience in business development or similar role.
2. Communication: Exceptional verbal and written English communication skills. You must be able to draft compelling, error-free proposals and emails.
The "Go-Getter" Spirit: A fast-paced, proactive mindset. You don’t wait for leads to come to you; you go out and find them.
3. Resilience: High energy and a "thick skin" for handling objections and following up until the deal is sealed.
4. Office-First: High discipline and commitment to a Work from Office environment in Bangalore.
 
Technical & Software Skills
- CRM Mastery: High proficiency in working with CRM systems (e.g., Salesforce, HubSpot, or Zoho CRM) to manage leads, track activities, and report on sales performance.
- Sales Prospecting Tools: Expert-level use of LinkedIn Sales Navigator and other lead-gen tools (e.g., Apollo.io, Lusha, or Seamless.ai).
- Communication Suites: Professional proficiency in Google Workspace or Microsoft Office (especially Excel/Sheets for tracking and PowerPoint/Slides for pitching).
- Sales Automation: Experience with email automation and sequencing tools is a strong plus.


Why Join Us?
At Appriffy, you aren't just selling a service; you are selling the future of work. You will help companies save over 50 hours of engineering time per hire while providing them with Silicon Valley-calibre talent.

Apply directly here or send your profile to shakir.mallick@appriffy.com

If you think the Web3 job market is dead, you are simply looking in the wrong repositories.

The "wild west" era of blockchain hiring is permanently over, replaced by a brutal demand for enterprise-grade infrastructure. The industry is no longer funding tourists; it is aggressively hunting for engineers who can actually scale distributed systems.

Right now is the single best time to enter Web3, but only if you bring serious engineering weight to the table. Writing basic token contracts will not secure you a position in 2026.

The massive capital has moved entirely into the protocol layer. Here is why your existing backend architecture skills provide the ultimate leverage:
The Go Advantage: AI easily handles boilerplate Solidity today. The real talent war is heavily focused on systems languages like Go to build the resilient peer-to-peer networking nodes that actually keep the network alive.

Deep State Management: Startups are hitting extreme database bottlenecks. Knowing how to tune distributed NoSQL engines—like CassandraDB—for high-throughput consensus data is what actually clears modern technical interviews.

Institutional Standards: Massive traditional finance firms are adopting decentralized rails. They demand absolute fault tolerance, turning deep infrastructure knowledge into the most lucrative asset on the market.

Switching to Web3 today requires treating it exactly like a traditional, high-stakes distributed systems role. If you can build the underlying plumbing, the market is completely yours for the taking.

What core backend concept are you currently sharpening to prepare for your next technical interview?

What I am seeing in the data and analytics job market is not a contradiction. It is a market that can look active at the headline level while still feeling structurally slow and difficult for experienced professionals trying to get hired. In March 2026, U.S. employers added 178,000 jobs and the unemployment rate held at 4.3%, which on the surface suggests resilience. However, aggregate payroll growth does not automatically translate into fast, decisive hiring for specialized knowledge work (Robert Half, 2026).
 
A closer reading of the labor market helps explain why so many candidates are experiencing delays, silence, and recruiter-heavy pipelines. According to Robert Half’s March 2026 analysis, professional and business services added only 2,000 jobs in March, while financial activities lost 15,000 jobs. At the same time, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported that job openings were little changed at 6.9 million in February, but hires fell to 4.8 million, with the hires rate declining to 3.1%, the lowest level since April 2020. That combination matters. It suggests that organizations may still have positions posted and labor needs to address, but they are moving with substantially less commitment and velocity than the headline payroll number implies. 
This is why so many job seekers, particularly in data, analytics, and adjacent professional fields, are seeing a disproportionate number of opportunities routed through staffing firms rather than directly through permanent employers. Employers appear to be preserving flexibility, outsourcing screening, and delaying final commitment even when the work itself is real.

Robert Half’s broader 2026 hiring research reinforces that interpretation: 60% of employers planned to add permanent staff by mid-2026, 55% expected to bring on contract talent, and only 6% of hiring managers said their organizations already had the talent needed to complete high-priority projects. In other words, the issue is not always the absence of demand. Increasingly, it is the gap between demand and decision-making confidence.
 
For experienced professionals, that distinction is critical. A recruiter-saturated job feed can create the appearance of momentum while concealing how selective and hesitant employers have become behind the scenes. That helps explain why so many processes appear to stall after initial contact: the labor market is still functioning, but it is functioning with friction. In this environment, visibility should not be confused with hiring intent, and posted demand should not be confused with organizational readiness to act. #ai #data #tech #technology #hiring #employment

In multiple cases, men were wrongfully arrested due to AI misidentification. Courts raised Fourth Amendment concerns.

A New Mexico jury ordered Meta to pay $375 million for failing to protect children.

This is not theory.

This is case law.

If your vendor tuned proxy variables to balance statistics, you pass the bias audit.

You still face constitutional risk.

Equal Protection claims focus on intent and structure.

Not only numbers.

So what do you do?

Add a constitutional screen before deployment:

• Review training data sources and proxy variables  
• Document design choices and risk tradeoffs  
• Require vendor disclosures on model logic  
• Add contract terms shifting liability back to the vendor  
• Run pre and post deployment assessments  

This moves you from math compliance to legal survival.

You avoid the mistake of assuming the audit equals safety.

Start simple.

What constitutional risks have you tested for beyond statistical bias?

3️⃣ Shadow AI — personal AI accounts used for work. IT has no idea.
4️⃣ No human oversight on AI decisions — hiring, lending, healthcare = regulatory nightmare
5️⃣ Forgetting deletion rights — customer data deleted must include AI training data too

I'm not anti-AI. I use it every day.
But I am pro-accountability.

Here's your 3-step fix to start today:
🔗 Never input confidential data into public AI tools
🔗 Use enterprise-grade AI with clear data agreements
🔗 Train your team — human error is still the #1 risk

The next big data scandal won't be a breach.
It'll be what AI quietly learned about all of us.

AI won't slow down.
Your data privacy strategy can't afford to either.

AI Scribes Are Working Exactly as Intended. That's the Problem.

AI medical scribes were supposed to be the easy win. A microphone listens to your doctor's appointment, an AI writes up the clinical notes, and the physician gets to spend less time staring at a screen and more time actually talking to you. Everyone claps. Burnout goes down. Efficiency goes up.

Except now insurers are publicly sounding the alarm that these tools are driving up healthcare costs. Blue Cross Blue Shield published a study in March estimating $2.3 billion in additional spending tied to AI-enabled billing practices, and Centene's CEO called out hospitals on earnings calls for coding emergency room fevers as sepsis. Meanwhile, hospitals privately agree that AI scribes are increasing coding intensity but have no incentive to stop. A Peterson Health Technology Institute roundtable earlier this year apparently went something like: yes, AI scribes are increasing coding intensity. One hundred percent. Then the hospital side went back to business as usual.

The mechanics are simple. AI scribes generate more detailed notes than most doctors would write on their own. More detailed notes capture more billable codes. More billable codes mean higher reimbursements from insurers. Hospitals love this because they've been arguing for years that they were being underpaid. Insurers hate it because the bills are going up and they're the ones holding the bag. Patients don't even know this fight is happening. They're just going to see the result on their next premium increase.

And the butterfly effect is worth following. Insurers eating higher costs have to make it up somewhere, which means raising premiums, cutting vendor contracts, tightening staff, or all three. Businesses paying those higher premiums have less room to hire. And when an AI tool makes it trivially easy to generate maximally billable documentation, the line between "more thorough charting" and "fraud, waste, and abuse" gets blurry fast.

One microphone in an exam room, and suddenly you're pulling on a thread that touches insurance markets, employment decisions, and federal billing compliance. Nobody in that chain lost their job to AI. But if an insurer lays off adjusters to offset rising costs, or a small business freezes hiring because their premiums went up 15%, those are still AI-driven job losses. They just won't show up in any headline about automation. The direct displacement story is easy to track. The second and third order effects are where things get interesting, and almost nobody is watching for them.
